title: Apple frangipane tart
subtitle: A buttery almond cream tart topped with thinly sliced apple
excerpt: A crisp sweet pastry case filled with almond frangipane, topped with overlapping thin slices of apple baked until the frangipane is set and fragrant and the apple edges are lightly caught. Elegant and straightforward to make.
sourceNotes: Frangipane tarts are documented in French and English baking literature from the eighteenth century. The almond cream filling (crème frangipane) appears in early French pastry manuals and is noted in English adaptations in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. The combination of frangipane with seasonal fruit is a standard of British and French patisserie, with apple as the autumn fruit of choice.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry case can be blind baked a day ahead and stored at room temperature. Bake the filled tart on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; conventional 190°C for the filled tart. Pastry blind-baked at 180°C fan.

glossaryTerms.definition: A baked almond cream filling made from butter, sugar, eggs, and ground almonds. It puffs slightly as it bakes and sets to a moist, dense crumb.
Baking a pastry case without its filling, weighed down with baking paper and beans or weights, to set the base before the filling is added.

content.text: Frangipane tarts work because the almond cream filling is forgiving and versatile. The

puffs around whatever fruit sits on top of it and holds the fruit in place as it bakes. Apple is the natural autumn partner — thinly sliced eating apples fan across the surface, their edges taking colour while the frangipane fills in around them and sets beneath.

before the filling goes in. This ensures a dry, crisp base that doesn't absorb moisture from the frangipane. The filled tart bakes at a moderate temperature: too hot and the frangipane souffles and collapses rather than setting evenly.

attrs.prepNote: cold, cut into pieces, for the pastry; plus 100 g softened for the frangipane
large eating apples, peeled, cored, and thinly sliced
juice only, to toss the apple

content.text: Make the pastry and blind bake

Rub the cold pastry-quantity {{unsalted-butter}} into {{plain-flour}} and {{icing-sugar}} until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Add {{egg-yolks}} and 1–2 tablespoons of cold water; mix to a firm dough. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Roll out and line a 23 cm loose-bottomed tart tin. Refrigerate for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Line with baking paper and fill with baking beans. Blind bake for 15 minutes, then remove paper and beans and bake for 5 minutes more until the base is dry and pale. Leave to cool.

Reduce the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Beat the frangipane-quantity softened {{unsalted-butter}} with {{caster-sugar}} until pale and combined. Beat in {{eggs}} one at a time, then fold in {{ground-almonds}} and {{vanilla-extract}}. Toss {{apple}} slices with the juice from {{lemon}} to prevent browning. Spread the frangipane evenly in the blind-baked case. Arrange the apple slices over the top in overlapping concentric rings or a fan pattern, pressing them lightly into the frangipane. Bake for 35–40 minutes until the frangipane is set and springs back when lightly pressed, and the apple edges are golden. If using, brush the warm tart with strained {{apricot-jam}} to glaze. Cool for 30 minutes before slicing.
A Cox, Braeburn, or Granny Smith gives the right balance of sweetness and sharpness. Avoid very sweet dessert apples, which can become jammy and lose definition.
To check the frangipane is baked through, insert a skewer into the centre: it should come out with moist crumbs, not wet batter.
The tart can be varied with pear, plum, or quince. Stone fruits benefit from a light poach before use if they are very firm.
